metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC5) that asserts findings produced by the two new antipattern paths carry the same required evidence-record fields as existing findings (e.g. by extending `test_code_review_evals.py` to check output shape for the new recall cases).
findings.finding: The plan body states 'evidence-record contract unchanged' as a required constraint on the new rubric text, but no AC verifies it — no structural check, no test asserting finding-output shape against the existing schema.
findings.location: AC1 (phrasing condition) and What section ('evidence-record contract unchanged')
findings.suggested_fix: In AC5, add a note: 'and assert each finding in the new recall cases includes non-empty `location`, `evidence`, and `checklist_item` fields matching the existing schema.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'or the legacy module path' hedge in AC #3 by stating the single canonical command (e.g., commit to one module path and note the other as a fallback only if the primary import fails, with the exact fallback spelled out).
findings.finding: AC #3's proving command contains an unresolved branch ('or the legacy module path') that prevents the executor from deterministically running the verification without first investigating which module path is current — a defaultable gap that should be resolved in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #3: `python -m rebar.llm.prompting.prompts regenerate-index (or the legacy module path)`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with a concrete two-liner that tries the primary and falls back deterministically: `python -m rebar.llm.prompting.prompts regenerate-index 2>/dev/null || python -m rebar.llm.prompts regenerate-index` — or pick the one that exists and delete the hedge.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the single `grep -c 'A\|B\|C\|D'` command in AC #2 with four individual grep checks (one per ID, each asserted ≥ 1) so each eval-case ID is independently verified present.
findings.finding: AC #2's `grep -c 'A\|B\|C\|D' file` returns 4 counts matching LINES (not unique pattern occurrences), so the assertion can pass with fewer than 4 distinct IDs (if one ID appears on two lines) or fail for a correct file (if two IDs share a line) — a cardinality assertion defect that makes the proving command unreliable.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC #2 with: `for id in R-non-gating-skip R-fault-wrong-seam FP-strict-xfail-with-reason FP-sleep-as-secondary; do grep -q "$id" src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-tests.eval.yaml || echo "MISSING: $id"; done` and assert no MISSING lines — or four separate `grep -c` calls each asserting >= 1.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a recall eval case for the bare non-strict xfail antipattern (e.g. `R-non-gating-xfail`: a diff adding `@pytest.mark.xfail` with no `strict=True` and no `reason` citing a removal condition) and a recall eval case for the sleep-primary synchronization antipattern (e.g. `R-sleep-only-sync`: a race test whose only synchronization is `time.sleep`), so every independently-named flaggable path in the new rubric text is covered by at least one recall case.
findings.finding: The plan names non-strict xfail (bare, no reason) and sleep-only synchronization as independently flaggable antipattern variants in the rubric text, but specifies no recall eval case exercising either of these two distinct fire paths — only a no-fire guard for each — leaving the model's ability to flag these specific patterns unverified at eval time.
findings.location: What > Antipatterns to add / Eval coverage to add in code-review-tests.eval.yaml
findings.suggested_fix:
Add two entries to `code-review-tests.eval.yaml`:
1. id: R-non-gating-xfail, mode: non-gating-test, diff: a test with `@pytest.mark.xfail` (no `strict=True`, no `reason` naming a removal condition), expected: flag
2. id: R-sleep-only-sync, mode: unrealistic-fault-injection, diff: a concurrency test whose only synchronization is `time.sleep(0.1)` with no barrier/event/marker-file, expected: flag
Also add both ids to the AC grep pattern in criterion 2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-llm-prompts.eval.yaml` as a consumer of `code-review-tests.md` and confirm its five diff hunks remain valid after the new FLAG/FP-guard sections are inserted.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-llm-prompts.eval.yaml` is an unenumerated consumer of `code-review-tests.md`: it contains five synthetic diffs whose `--- a/` / `+++ b/` headers name `code-review-tests.md` directly (lines 34–35, 77–78, 106–107, 119–120, 132–133). The plan does not enumerate this file, so an implementer won't check whether the additive sections land in a position that invalidates any of those diff hunks.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the plan: 'Verify `code-review-llm-prompts.eval.yaml` diff hunks targeting `code-review-tests.md` are unaffected (new sections appended after existing content, not inserted mid-file).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a recall eval case (e.g. `R-sleep-only-race`) for the sleep-only synchronization sub-variant — a diff whose race test's only synchronization is `time.sleep(...)` with no barrier/event/marker-file — so the eval suite can score whether the model actually fires on this named antipattern.
findings.finding: The 'sleep-only synchronization' sub-variant of the unrealistic-fault-injection antipattern is named as independently flaggable in the rubric text, but the eval spec adds only a no-fire guard for its valid form (FP-sleep-as-secondary) with no corresponding recall case for the bad form (a race test whose SOLE synchronization is a timing sleep). Without a recall case, `test_code_review_dataset_balanced` cannot score whether the model actually catches sleep-only race tests; the rubric text and eval coverage are asymmetric.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the eval dataset:
```yaml
- id: R-sleep-only-race
  corpus: dso
  expect: finding
  mode: unrealistic-fault-injection
  diff: |
    --- a/tests/test_worker.py
    +++ b/tests/test_worker.py
    @@ -1,3 +1,8 @@
    +def test_concurrent_flush(worker):
    +    thread = threading.Thread(target=worker.flush)
    +    thread.start()
    +    time.sleep(0.1)  # only synchronization — no barrier/event/marker
    +    assert worker.flushed is True
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a recall eval case (e.g. `R-sleep-only-sync`, `mode: sleep-only-sync`, `expect: finding`) with a diff showing a race test whose ONLY synchronization mechanism is `time.sleep()`, to ensure the eval suite validates the (b) sub-variant of the unrealistic-fault-injection antipattern.
findings.finding: The plan's compound antipattern (b) — sleep-only synchronization — has no recall eval case: only R-fault-wrong-seam (covering sub-variant a: fault injected outside the seam) and FP-sleep-as-secondary (a no-fire guard) are specified. Without a recall case exercising the sleep-only variant, the eval suite never validates that the overlay actually flags it, leaving a coverage hole in the rubric surface the plan claims to own.
findings.location: Plan section 'Eval coverage to add in code-review-tests.eval.yaml' / 'Antipatterns to add to the FLAG list'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the dataset in code-review-tests.eval.yaml:
```yaml
- id: R-sleep-only-sync
  corpus: dso
  expect: finding
  mode: sleep-only-sync
  diff: |
    --- a/tests/test_worker.py
    +++ b/tests/test_worker.py
    @@ -1,5 +1,9 @@
    +def test_concurrent_writes_safe():
    +    results = []
    +    t = threading.Thread(target=lambda: results.append(worker.process()))
    +    t.start()
    +    time.sleep(0.2)  # sole synchronization — race-prone, no barrier/event/join
    +    assert len(results) == 1
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the R-non-gating-skip eval case embeds CI context evidence: either (a) embed a synthetic CI workflow snippet directly in the `diff:` payload (e.g. as a second diff hunk for `.github/workflows/ci.yml`) so the model can infer CI context from the diff alone without file-tool access, or (b) document that the eval solver's `files:` mechanism must be extended for the code-review arm to seed fixture CI files into the case repo.
findings.finding: The R-non-gating-skip recall eval case requires the overlay model to consult CI workflow files via its file tools (the whole point of the antipattern: the skip condition holds *in the project's CI*). But the eval_solver code-review arm (`_run_code_review_case`) injects only the `diff:` field as `ticket_context` and does NOT seed a disposable fixture repo — so the model has no CI workflow files to inspect. This means the 'fail-open' fallback ('when CI context is not determinable, do NOT flag') will always fire, making the R-non-gating-skip recall case systematically unscorable under the eval harness as specified.
findings.location: Plan section 'Eval coverage to add' — R-non-gating-skip case; src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_solver.py:123-153 (_run_code_review_case)
findings.suggested_fix:
Embed the CI workflow as a second diff hunk in the R-non-gating-skip diff payload, e.g.:
```yaml
diff: |
  --- a/.github/workflows/ci.yml
  +++ b/.github/workflows/ci.yml
  @@ -10,0 +11 @@
  +        python-version: '3.11'  # CI runs only on Linux/3.11
  --- a/tests/test_feature.py
  +++ b/tests/test_feature.py
  @@ -1,3 +1,7 @@
  +@pytest.mark.skipif(sys.platform != 'win32', reason='Windows-only')
  +def test_feature_windows():
  +    assert feature() == expected
```
This makes the skip condition and CI context co-present in the diff so the model does not need file tools to resolve them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the evaluation order for the non-gating test flag rule in the plan: CI-context resolution must be attempted FIRST; if context is not determinable, stop (fail-open, no flag); only if CI context is resolved should the skip-condition check proceed.
findings.finding: The decision protocol for the non-gating antipattern embeds a CI-resolution step ('the reviewer resolves the project's CI from repo context') but leaves the sequencing of that step unspecified relative to the flag decision: the overlay instruction could be interpreted as 'check skip condition in diff first, then verify CI context' OR 'check CI context first, then check skip condition'. The plan never specifies the order, which means two conforming implementations of the overlay text could produce different verdicts on a diff where the skip is visible but CI context is ambiguous.
findings.location: Plan section 'Antipatterns to add — non-gating test' guard definition

findings.finding: AC1 has no mechanical proving command — the criterion that both antipatterns and guard sets appear in the correct sections relies on visual inspection, with no grep or diff anchor to produce evidence on success.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: `grep -c 'non-gating test' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-tests.md && grep -c 'unrealistic fault injection' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-tests.md && grep -c 'false-positive' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/code-review-tests.md` (adjust anchors to match actual section headings) — all must return ≥ 1.

findings.finding: The non-gating test guard references 'the supported CI environment' as a decision criterion (e.g. 'a skip guarding a genuinely optional platform/dependency that the supported CI environment DOES exercise is NOT a finding'), but this phrase is undefined in the plan, in `code-review-tests.md`, and has no definition anywhere in the codebase. The executing LLM reviewer has no mechanism to resolve what CI environment the project uses, making the guard an ambiguous oracle that cannot be applied consistently.
findings.location: Plan section: 'non-gating test' antipattern, guard clause
